ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general kebab food product class; no specific Turkish shish kebab entry exists. Closest parent match used.
ontology.foodex2_description: Meat preparations — skewered and grilled meat (kebab-type)
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a dedicated Turkish shish kebab code; A0FKP (meat preparations, grilled skewered) is the closest defensible anchor.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for Turkish shish kebab as a food concept.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is approximately 300 g, comprising grilled lamb skewers (approximately 150–180 g) served with mandi rice (approximately 120–150 g), as typically plated in a Singapore hawker or restaurant context.

nutrient_source.title: Turkish Shish Kebab — HPB Singapore Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Turkish Shish Kebab (cr_id G00010355, confidence 0.95). Saturated fat not reported by HPB; estimated at 9.5 g/100 g based on USDA FoodData Central entry for lamb, ground, cooked (USDA FDC ID 174351) as a compositional proxy for the lamb component, documented in notes.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Turkish shish kebab as a composite dish. The dish is predominantly lamb protein and fat with a small carbohydrate fraction (5.24 g/100 g from marinade and rice component). GI estimated at 45 (low) based on the analogous mixed meat-and-rice dish profile from the University of Sydney GI Database; the rice component (mandi rice, long-grain) has a GI of approximately 50–55, but the overall dish GI is moderated by the dominant protein and fat content. GL per 300 g serving = (45 × 5.24 × 3) / 100 = 7.1 rounded to 3.5 per 100 g equivalent portion of the kebab component alone. Estimation is conservative and documented.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 1080 mg/100 g — far exceeds the 600 mg/100 g upper threshold for hypertension-safe foods
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid or strictly limit Turkish shish kebab as prepared; a 150 g serving delivers approximately 1620 mg sodium, exceeding the WHO daily recommended limit of 2000 mg in a single dish, and the marinade salt should be replaced with herbs and spices such as cumin, paprika, and oregano.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low carbohydrate (5.24 g/100 g) and low sugar (2.85 g/100 g) produce minimal glycaemic impact, but high total fat (26.4 g/100 g) and high sodium (1080 mg/100 g) require portion control
type2_diabetes.guidance: The kebab component itself has a low glycaemic impact due to minimal carbohydrate content; however, individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should moderate portion size to manage caloric and fat intake, avoid high-GI accompaniments such as white rice or white pita bread, and be cautious of the high sodium content which is associated with cardiovascular risk in T2D.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High total fat (26.4 g/100 g), estimated saturated fat (~9.5 g/100 g), high sodium (1080 mg/100 g), and cholesterol (85 mg/100 g) collectively elevate cardiovascular risk
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit consumption of Turkish shish kebab due to its high saturated fat and sodium content; substituting lamb with lean chicken or fish, reducing marinade salt, and limiting portion size to 100–120 g are recommended risk-reduction strategies.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Red meat (lamb) is a primary arachidonic acid source amplifying inflammatory pathways; estimated saturated fat ~9.5 g/100 g exceeds the 5 g/100 g RA threshold; sodium at 1080 mg/100 g worsens RA-related tissue swelling
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid or minimise lamb-based shish kebab; substituting with chicken or oily fish reduces arachidonic acid load, and marinade salt should be reduced to lower sodium-driven tissue inflammation.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Turkish Shish Kebab is a classic Middle Eastern grilled dish consisting of small cubes or pieces of marinated lamb threaded onto metal or wooden skewers and cooked over an open charcoal or gas flame. The marinade typically includes olive oil, garlic, cumin, paprika, oregano, salt, and lemon juice, which tenderise the meat and impart a smoky, aromatic flavour. In the Singapore hawker and restaurant context, the dish is commonly served with mandi rice — a fragrant long-grain rice cooked with spices — along with grilled tomatoes, bell peppers, and onions. The texture of the kebab is firm yet juicy when freshly grilled, with a charred exterior and a savoury, mildly spiced interior. Nutritionally, the dish is high in total fat (26.4 g/100 g) and saturated fat (estimated ~9.5 g/100 g), moderate in protein (10.7 g/100 g), and low in carbohydrates (5.24 g/100 g), making it glycaemically benign but calorically dense at 302 kcal/100 g. The sodium content is very high at 1080 mg/100 g, which is a significant concern for individuals with hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The dish is naturally gluten-free and halal. It is not suitable for individuals with hypertension or rheumatoid arthritis due to its sodium and saturated fat content, and should be consumed in moderation by those with Type 2 Diabetes or cardiovascular disease.

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id G00010355, confidence 0.95, review_status reviewed). Saturated fat estimated from USDA FoodData Central lamb proxy and documented. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database framework, clearly documented.
notes: 1. Saturated fat (9.5 g/100 g) is estimated using USDA FoodData Central entry for cooked ground lamb (FDC ID 174351) as a compositional proxy for the lamb component of this dish; HPB does not report saturated fat for this entry. 2. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions, micronutrient panel, and moisture data for this composite Turkish shish kebab dish are not available in the HPB Singapore Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central as a matched composite entry as of 2026-04-16. 3. dietary_fibre_g is reported as 0.0 by HPB; this is a confirmed zero per the source. 4. GI of 45 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for Turkish shish kebab exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. 5. The dish description notes it comes with mandi rice; nutrient values in per_100g reflect the composite dish as reported by HPB. 6. Halal status is inferred from the dish composition (lamb, no pork, no alcohol in standard preparation) and its listing in the HPB Singapore food database context.
